name: Mock Oral Exam
answers_pdf: /Users/seeratjaura/Downloads/Old ABA Exams - Answers and Study Guide.pdf

session_stems.exam1_session2:
SESSION 2 – 35 Minutes (total time)
A 20-year-old 60 kg, 5'4" tall primigravida is scheduled for urgent Cesarean delivery at 37 weeks gestation.  She
presented one hour ago in labor and is pre-eclamptic.  She has received one dose of hydralazine and magnesium
sulfate.  She admits to frequent heroin use.  The obstetrician has just notified you that late decelerations of FHR are
occurring.  P 110, BP 170/110 mmHg, R 22, T 37.5o C, Hgb 9.8 gm/dL.

mappings.question_text: Pre-eclampsia: What are the anesthetic implications of pre-eclampsia? Why is the fetus at increased risk when pre-eclampsia occurs? Volume status of preeclamptic patient? Is this patient adequately treated? Additional therapy? Endpoint to Rx of BP?
mappings.answer_text:
Anesthetic implications of preeclampsia: Neuro (seizures, stroke), cardiac (HTN leading
to stroke, MI, CHF, labile BPs intravascular depletion; intravascular depletion leading to
morepronounceddropinBPuponinduction), pulm (pulmonary edema, airway edema
making intubation more challenging), GI (elevated LFTs, HELLP syndrome), renal (AKI),
heme(thrombocytopenia), MSK (patient is on Mg, which can potentiate nondepolarizing
neuromuscular blockade for case done under GA; avoid NMBDs if possible; Mg does
NOTpotentiate the effects of succinylcholine), practical (more difficult IV access 2/2
edema). In preeclampsia, there is abnormal formation of the uterine placental spiral
arterioles, leading to impaired placental bloodflow and increased risk for fetal HR
decelerations/impaired perfusion to the fetus. This can lead to IUGR. Volume status of
preeclamptic patient is intravascularly depleted. The BP goals for a preeclamptic patient
are for BP < 160/110. BPs greater than 160/110 put mom at high risk of stroke and
intracranial hemorrhage. However, you wouldn’t want to normalize the BP or drop it too
low because this would lead to uteroplacental insufficiency.
Ecclampsia seizures: loading dose of magnesium 4-6 g over 20 minutes followed by an
infusion of 2 g/hr. Administer a benzo/antiseizure med, support airway, administer
anti-HTN meds as needed.

mappings.question_text: FHR - late decelerations: Significance of late decelerations? Other diagnostic tests of fetal distress? Is the next 30 minutes better spent medically treating mother or delivering baby? Why?
mappings.answer_text:
Late decels = uteroplacental insufficiency. Other diagnostic tests of fetal distress include
fetal scalp HR tracing and fetal scalp pH. Timing of delivery would be discussed with the
OB;wouldideally have some time to hydrate mom and manage BP better, but if OB is
saying that this case needs to go now, then i would not delay the C/S.

mappings.question_text: Heroin use: Last heroin use was 24 hours ago. How will this affect anesthetic mgmt? How will this affect fetus? Affect neonate?
mappings.answer_text:
Heroin use will mean that mom may be prone to withdrawal (increase in HR and BP) and
mayhavepostoperative pain that is more difficult to treat 2/2 tolerance. She also may be
amoredifficult IV access. The fetus may also develop opioid dependence, and thus after
delivery, the neonate may show signs of acute intoxication (apnea/hypopnea, sluggish)
or withdrawal (irritability, tachycardia, HTN). A neonatologist should be present at the
time of delivery to evaluate the neonate.

mappings.question_text: Preanesthetic management: No blood typed and crossed. Would you delay procedure until blood is available? What if antibodies are complicating cross-match? Additional lab tests required? Which? Why? Preop medications? Explain.
mappings.answer_text:
I would discuss the urgency of this C/S with the OB, and I would also ask the patient
about any prior h/o transfusions. If she has never been transfused, she is unlikely to
have developed major antibodies as a P0. If the blood type was back but antibodies
(presumably non-major antibodies) were complicating the process, then i would ask the
OBhowlongwecoulddelaytheC/S,ifatall. If the OB did not believe that we could wait
any longer for the C/S, then i would proceed with the C/S and administer the
type-matched blood without knowing the specific minor antibodies (eg, Duff, Kell, etc)
until the antibody identification process was completed, as the likelihood of a major
adverse transfusion reaction from these minor antibodies is low. Preop, give sodium
citrate, famotidine, metoclopramide. The only lab test that I would want is the platelet
count.

mappings.question_text: Monitoring: Do you need a CVP catheter for procedure? Why/why not? Possible complications that are more likely to occur associated with CVP placement because of her medical history?
mappings.answer_text:
Don’t need a CVP. Given the patient’s intravascular depletion, she may be more likely to
have a compressible internal jugular vein that is more readily punctured through the back
wall. She may also have a higher chance of bleeding/hematoma 2/2 thrombocytopenia
andimpaired platelet function.

mappings.question_text: Anesthesia choice - regional vs. general: Is spinal anesthesia an appropriate choice? Why/why not? Epidural better? Benefits vs. risk of general anesthesia in this patient? Explain.
mappings.answer_text:
Spinal is appropriate as long as her platelet count is normal. Epidural would be helpful
for managing postoperative pain, which may be more difficult to manage in this patient
whoisachronic heroin user.

mappings.question_text: Induction of general anesthesia: Patient insists on general anesthesia. Assume normal airway. Problems anticipated during induction? How will you treat/avoid? Likelihood of intra-op awareness? Can you avoid? How?
mappings.answer_text:
Anticipated problems on induction: aspiration risk (pregnancy), difficult airway
(pregnancy, preeclampsia), rapid desaturation (reduced FRC, higher O2 consumption),
morelabile BP prone to dropping significantly with induction (intravascular depletion from
poorly controlled HTN). Pretreatment meds for aspiration risk, have difficult airway
equipment in room, elevated HOB to reduce aspiration risk, preoxygenate well, use
etomidate for induction and have vasopressors readily available, pre-induction arterial
line can be considered if time permits. Higher likelihood of intraoperative awareness in
obstetrical surgery, but can never guarantee with absolute certainty that this patient
won’t have it. Can take steps to reduce the risk of intraoperative awareness by using a
BIS monitor, administering midazolam after delivery, paying close attention to the
monitor for signs of tachycardia/HTN suggestive of light anesthesia, don’t use a
long-acting paralytic agent so that I could look for patient movement as another sign of
light anesthesia.

mappings.question_text: Severe post-induction hypertension: Following intubation, BP 240/140 mmHg. Possible adverse consequences to mother, fetus? How treat?
mappings.answer_text:
Severe HTN can lead to stroke, intracranial hemorrhage, and MI in mom, leading to
reduced CO which would decrease perfusion to the placenta, which can lead to
insufficient fetal perfusion. Treat with deepening the anesthetic, administer a quick-acting
andshort-acting anti-HTN like clevidipine, nitroglycerin, or esmolol.

mappings.question_text: Neonatal resuscitation: BP controlled, mother stable. Colleague relieves you to care for neonate. Apgar 2 with thick meconium. How will you proceed? Will you give naloxone? Why/why not?
mappings.answer_text:
Apgar 2 with thick meconium → assess respiratory effort and HR. If apneic/gasping with
HR<100,initiate PPV (mask ventilation) at a rate of 40-60 breaths/min or CPAP and
monitor with SpO2 and EKG. If the HR is NOT increasing, evaluate for adequate PPV
andseal, suction nose/mouth, consider intubating. If the HR <60, intubate the neonate. If
the HR increases >100, can stop PPV and switch to supplemental O2. If the HR
increases but remains <100, ensure adequate chest rise with PPV.
Neonatal resuscitation algorithm - 2010 guidelines
After delivery of a neonate with suspected meconium aspiration syndrome, do NOT
perform vigorous suctioning. The care for these neonates should follow the same
general principles for further intervention (eg, intubation), which are based on
inadequate respiratory effort (gasping, labored breathing, poor oxygenation) or HR (<100
beats/min). The management of meconium aspiration syndrome is largely supportive:
maintenance of adequate oxygenation and ventilation; maintenance of adequate BP and
perfusion; correction of metabolic/glucose abnormalities; empiric antibiotic therapy;
minimal handling of the infant to avoid agitation, which exacerbates persistent pulmonary
hypertension of the newborn (PPHN) if present. For newborns with MAS causing severe
respiratory compromise with PPHN, they may need mechanical ventilation,
high-frequency ventilation, inhaled nitric oxide, surfactant therapy, and/or ECMO.
Don’t give naloxone because this would just put the neonate into withdrawal.

mappings.question_text: Severe hemorrhage: Neonatologist arrives to care for neonate. During extraction of placenta, severe hemorrhage occurs. How proceed? Transfuse? Reason(s). What if cross-match not complete?
mappings.answer_text:
Severe hemorrhage with placental extraction ddx: morbidly adherent placenta (i.e.,
acreta vs increta vs percreta), atony, blood vessel injury/laceration. Look for bigger IV
access, place an arterial line if one isn’t already in place, call for more blood and
consider MTP if there’s continued massive hemorrhage, ask the OB about uterine tone
andconsider administering more oxytocin IV, misoprostol rectally, carboprost IM (avoid
methylergonovine 2/2 preeclampsia), consider calling IR for possible uterine artery
embolization. Transfuse if there is continued bleeding and hemodynamic instability, but if
there is hemostasis in the surgical field and the patient remains hemodynamically stable,
then i would use an ABG to check for a Hct to guide transfusion decision. If cross-match
not complete, would give O-negative blood.

mappings.question_text: Posterior fossa craniotomy: A 58-yr-old man is to undergo craniotomy while in the sitting position for a posterior fossa tumor. Is isoflurane the preferred anesthetic for this procedure? Why/why not? Is N O useful in 2 this procedure? Advantages/disadvantages? Is CVP essential? Why/why not? How verify correct placement? Can increase in ICP be prevented? How? Is mannitol more effective than furosemide to reduce ICP? Why/why not? Is there a limit to how low PaCO should be taken? Explain. How will you differentiate brain stem 2 ischemia from venous air embolism? Are there similarities? Explain.
mappings.answer_text:
Avoid using isoflurane due to its slower emergence time, as these patients generally
have a neuro exam at the end of the case for which the surgeons would want an
awake/alert patient prior to extubating. Avoid N2O given the tendency for N2O to migrate
into closed air spaces, which can occur once the skull is closed and lead to
pneumocephalus. CVP is essential due to high risk of VAE. Placement confirmed by
TEE,orseeing biphasic P waves on EKG with wire and then pulling wire back one cm.
Increased ICP can be prevented by avoiding N2O and volatile anesthetics, using an
anesthetic agent that lowers CBF and ICP (eg, propofol), and asking the surgeons to
place an EVD at the end of the case to monitor ICP and to potentially drain CSF as
needed. Mannitol and furosemide are equally effective osmotic diuretics that also reduce
the production of CSF. The PaCO2 should be lowered only as low as PaCO2 25-30
mmHg,anditseffects on ICP reduction are ephemeral (1-24 hours in duration).
Hyperventilation can be useful for elevated ICP due to cerebral edema, intracranial
hemorrhage, and tumor, but hyperventilation should be minimized in patients with stroke
or a traumatic brain injury, as the vasoconstriction induced by hyperventilation may
cause a decrease in local cerebral perfusion, resulting in worsening neurologic injury.
Certain brainstem strokes (lateral medullary syndrome 2/2 ischemia to posterior inferior
cerebellar artery->ischemia lateral medulla oblongata in the brainstem) share similarities
to VAE: they cause autonomic dysfunction causing tachycardia, labile blood
pressure, intermittent bradycardia. Differentiate brainstem ischemia (changes in
evoked potentials, impaired brainstem reflexes (eg, cough and gag reflexes,
vestibulo-oculocephalic reflex, pupillary reactivity, corneal reflex)) from venous air
embolus (see air on a TEE, hear turbulent flow with a precordial doppler, hemodynamic
compromise with a drop in BP and EtCO2).

mappings.question_text: Pain management: A 68-year-old woman is referred to you with acute herpes zoster and pain involving the T6 – T7 dermatomes on the right. She has COPD requiring nasal oxygen. How does her COPD affect your therapeutic options? Explain. What therapy would you offer her? Role of antiviral medications? Does your Rx plan differ if the herpes zoster is chronic vs. acute? Explain.
mappings.answer_text:
COPDmakesyoumorehesitanttoprescribeasystemicmedication that could affect
respiratory drive (eg, opiates). Antiviral therapy (eg, acyclovir, valacyclovir, famciclovir) is
offered if the patient presents within 72 hours of the development of symptoms. If
symptomsbegan>72hoursagobutnewvesicles/lesions are still appearing, then
antivirals are also offered to that patient population. If symptoms began >72 hours ago
andnomorenewlesionsareappearing,there’s unknown benefit to beginning antivirals,
so wouldn’t recommend it.
This patient has acute herpes zoster (reactivation of latent varicella-zoster virus (VZV)
along a sensory ganglion) with pain and rash adhering to a dermatomal pattern.
Analgesic options include NSAIDs, acetaminophen, tramadol, opioids. For patients with
uncomplicated acute herpes zoster (i.e., not a chronic pain syndrome that continues
after the resolution of the rash and vesicles), there is NO role for adjuvant meds like
gabapentin, TCAs, or glucocorticoids; no good evidence that backs the use of these
medstoprevent postherpetic neuralgia (PHN).
For patients with complicated zoster (i.e., ocular, otic, or neurologic manifestations), IV
antivirals are used and glucocorticoids may have a role.
herpeszosterpainsyndrome(postherpeticneuralgia)-painthatpersists>4monthsinthesame
distribution as a preceding documented episode of acute herpes zoster, even after resolution of
theassociated rash/vesicles.
a) burning,sharp,orstabbing paininthoracic,cervical, and trigeminal nerves are most
commonlyaffected;allodynia(painfromatypicallynon-painfulstimulus)
b) Initial treatment for moderate to severe pain (acute event): gabapentin or pregabalin
(avoid in pts w CKD->use TCAs)
i)      TCAs(amitriptyline) - anticholinergic side effect profile (eg, sedation, dry
mouth),sousewithcautionintheelderly
ii)      Othertreatmentoptions:topicalcapsaicin
(1) Intrathecal glucocorticoid injection if pain intractable
(2) Noothermeds,cryotherapy,TENS,topicallidocaine,orsurgeryhave
enoughevidence

mappings.question_text: AICD: A 60-year-old patient with an AICD (automatic implantable cardiac defibrillator) in place presents for exploratory laparotomy for suspected acute appendicitis. Anesthetic implications? Should the device be disarmed? Why/why not? Should succinylcholine be avoided? If so, why? How will you proceed? Rationale.
mappings.answer_text:
Succinylcholine fasciculations may be interpreted as pacing spikes, leading to inhibition
of the pacing function of an AICD, so prior to giving sux, you should reprogram the AICD
to asynchronous mode for a pacer-dependent person (putting a magnet on a patient with
anAICDonlydeactivates the shocking function). However, the AICD’s antiarrhythmia
capabilities usually are very sophisticated and precise, and so inappropriate AICD
discharge is unlikely.

unmatched_answers.answer_text:
Regional would be great: avoid the hemodynamic lability with GA, can use awake mental
status for determining adequacy of cerebral perfusion, she’s not a tough airway, so if she
